one_line_takeaway: RULES trains LLMs to explicitly check a lemma's preconditions before trusting its conclusion, using section-aware RL credit assignment that isolates blame to the responsible reasoning stage, yielding large robustness gains (up to ~23+ points) on precondition-breaking perturbations with parity or modest gains on end-to-end math benchmarks -- this is a mathematical-reasoning/RL-training paper with no security threat model, attack surface, or adversarial evaluation.
key_finding: Across all four base models, RULES improves in-domain lemma-judging accuracy over both Vanilla and single-label GRPO (e.g., DeepMath: NaturalProofs 60.8 vanilla -> 77.5 GRPO -> 88.5 RULES; IMO-lemma-derived test 31.9 -> 65.8 -> 89.5). On the out-of-domain deeptheorem-perturbation suite, RULES shows the largest relative gains, up to '23+ points' over baselines per the paper's own framing (e.g., DeepMath perturbation test: 24 vanilla -> 35.6 GRPO -> 47.0 RULES; OLMO2: 47.6 -> 46.8 -> 60.5). On end-to-end transfer benchmarks (Putnam-Axiom, CounterMATH, IneqMath, MATH-Perturb hard/simple, TheoremQA-Math), RULES is broadly on par with or modestly better than GRPO, with occasional regressions (e.g., Qwen2.5 CounterMATH/IM: 55 GRPO vs 53 RULES; DeepMath IM: 73 GRPO vs 71 RULES), which the authors attribute to a 'performance-rigorousness tradeoff' where RULES's strict precondition gating reduces cases where a model 'gets lucky' by hallucinating applicability. Ablations (on Qwen2.5-Math-7B-Instruct) show: (a) a 'two-section-onetime' variant (both judgments emitted together at the end rather than sequentially with section-aware masking) achieves strong in-domain accuracy but much weaker perturbation robustness -- RULES beats it by 14.7 points on the perturbation test (87.8 vs 73.1); (b) removing perturbation training data preserves in-domain scores but collapses perturbation-suite performance (19.1 vs RULES's 87.8); (c) 'de-noised' perturbation data (filtered by o3 + human validation) gives mixed results, still below RULES on the perturbation suite (74.3 vs 87.8), indicating a tradeoff between strict data cleaning and robustness coverage rather than clean labels uniformly helping.
core_contribution: The paper proposes RULES (Reliable Use of Lemmas via Eligibility Reasoning and Section-Aware Reinforcement Learning), a training framework for LLMs that reformulates 'lemma-judging' (deciding whether a candidate lemma is useful for proving a given statement) as a two-section structured-prediction task: a precondition check (do the lemma's hypotheses hold in the current statement?) and a conclusion-utility check (is the lemma's conclusion actually helpful for the proof goal?). The final usefulness label is the logical AND of the two section labels. Training uses GRPO with a novel 'section-aware loss masking' mechanism: when the aggregated final label is wrong but one section's prediction is known to be correct, the RL gradient/loss mask is applied only to the tokens of the responsible section (e.g., only the conclusion-check tokens), rather than penalizing the entire rollout. The authors also construct precondition-breaking perturbation training/eval data (minimally edited statements that invalidate a lemma's hypotheses while remaining mathematically coherent) to teach and test this 'check-before-use' discipline.
threat_model: N/A - this is a mathematical-reasoning/LLM-training paper, not a security paper. There is no adversary, attack surface, or threat model in the paper; 'robustness' here refers to robustness of lemma-applicability judgments against superficial/surface-level pattern matching, not adversarial or security robustness.
limitations: The paper's own Limitations section states: (1) task scope is restricted to lemma-judging rather than full proof generation, and the tailored supervision may bias models toward unexpected behaviors; (2) label coverage/noise -- perturbation data supplies only the precondition label (conclusion-utility label omitted to avoid strategy-dependence), introducing asymmetric supervision bias, and rewards are binary with a format penalty, which simplifies optimization but may underutilize graded signals; (3) model and data coverage is limited to four mid-sized models and English-only math corpora (NaturalProofs, NLPS, Isabelle premise selection, IMO-lemma), leaving gaps across math subfields and formal proof assistants (e.g., Lean, Coq), with multilingual/other-assistant extension left to future work.

datasets_used: NLPS / Natural Language Premise Selection (Ferreira and Freitas, 2020) - 2858 training examples
Premise Selection in Isabelle (Mikula et al., 2023) - 4484 training examples
In-domain test splits: naturalproofs-test (1002), nlps-test (676), isabelle-test (256), IMO-lemma-test (342)
deeptheorem-perturbation (839) - derived from DeepTheorem (Zhang et al., 2025), held-out out-of-domain perturbation suite
End-to-end benchmarks: Putnam-Axiom (Gulati et al., 2024), IneqMath (Sheng et al., 2025b), CounterMATH (Li et al., 2025b), MATH-Perturb (Huang et al., 2025), TheoremQA (Chen et al., 2023)
quantitative_results: Ablation (Qwen2.5-Math-7B, perturbation test): RULES 87.8 vs two-section-onetime 73.1 (14.7-point gap); vs w/o-perturbation-data 19.1; vs de-noised-perturbation-data 74.3
Perturbation training-data quality: 84.8% consensus between o3 and GPT-OSS-120B cross-validation; 90% human-verified pass rate (100-sample check)
Perturbation evaluation-data quality: 95% pass rate (100-sample o3-based and human-filtered check)
baselines_compared: Vanilla: direct inference with the two-section prompt schema, no RL fine-tuning
GRPO: standard GRPO reinforcement learning using only the final single (aggregated) usefulness label as reward signal, with the vanilla prompt
two-section-onetime (ablation): two-section schema but both precondition/conclusion judgments emitted together at the end rather than sequentially with section-aware masking
w/o perturbation data (ablation): RULES training with perturbation examples removed
de-noised perturbation data (ablation): perturbation training data filtered by o3 model and human validation for quality
